title: Release Reconciliation, Incident Response, and Production Defense · Language Lens: Feed Go Incident Command
summary: The team has many passing checks but cannot bind source to artifact, explain feed loss, prove publisher respect, restore service, or identify residual risks. Predict the reader decision, feed or API contract, Go and PostgreSQL ownership, bounded failure, and observable evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the feed go incident command increment in defensible production Go feed aggregator. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-cas…
objectives: Triage freshness, publisher overload, SSRF, parser, database, tenant, content, search, rollout, and restore incidents with evidence-preserving containment.
Demonstrate unfamiliar RSS and Atom sources from authorized discovery through bounded fetch, normalization, transaction, scheduling, API, accessible reader, and cleanup.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Feed aggregation and Go Predict Feed Go Incident Command · Theory Feed Go Incident Command
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go team handles case 5518 during an accessible community-news reader review. Build deterministic Go evidence to defend source-to-artifact provenance, unfamiliar end-to-end behavior, incidents, recovery, and residual risk; browser Go must remain deterministic and free of network, database, process, and host effects; then change one tenant, cursor, reader-state, authorization, cache, or accessibility condition without leaking state. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, sockets, XML libraries, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, race, load, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. For Feed aggregation and Go Defend the Feed Go Incident Command model · Theory Feed Go Incident Command, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: Restarting the service is the first response to every incident.
